title: Docker builds failing with 'no space left on device'
symptoms: docker build fails with 'write /var/lib/docker/tmp: no space left on device'
df -h shows /var/lib/docker at 100%
CI jobs start failing intermittently
root_cause: Accumulated dangling images, stopped containers and build cache
Large layers in multi-stage builds retained by BuildKit
Overlay2 driver writing to a volume without an auto-prune policy
diagnosis: docker system df to see disk usage by type
journalctl -u docker for any driver errors
fix: Reclaim space immediately:

```bash
docker system prune -af --volumes
docker builder prune -af
```
Move Docker root to a larger disk if needed:
Restart the daemon: `systemctl restart docker`
prevention: Schedule a nightly `docker system prune -af --filter until=72h`
Use `--mount=type=cache` in Dockerfiles instead of COPY caches
Monitor disk with a 75% alert threshold

title: Docker builds are extremely slow on every commit
symptoms: Every CI build takes 10+ minutes
Cache is never reused between runs
npm install or apt-get runs on every build
root_cause: COPY . . placed before dependency install invalidates cache
BuildKit not enabled so layers cannot be shared across runners
No remote cache backend configured
diagnosis: Inspect build output for 'CACHED' lines - if none appear cache is broken
fix: Reorder the Dockerfile to copy lock files first:

```dockerfile
FROM node:20-alpine
WORKDIR /app
COPY package*.json ./
RUN npm ci
COPY . .
RUN npm run build
```
Enable a registry cache:

```bash
docker buildx build \
  --cache-to type=registry,ref=reg/app:cache,mode=max \
  --cache-from type=registry,ref=reg/app:cache .
```
prevention: Adopt multi-stage builds
Pin base images and run Dependabot on them
Enforce image size budgets in CI

title: Containers exit immediately after starting without clear error message
symptoms: docker run or docker-compose up shows container exit code 1
docker logs shows minimal or no output
Container runs fine locally but fails in CI/CD environment
Restart policy causes continuous restart loop
root_cause: Main process in container completes execution (not a daemon process)
Missing required environment variables or configuration files
Entrypoint script failing silently without proper error handling
Healthcheck failing causing container to be marked unhealthy and stopped
diagnosis: Inspect exit code: docker inspect <container> | grep ExitCode
Check logs with --tail and --timestamps: docker logs --tail 100 <container>
Run container with interactive tty: docker run -it <image> /bin/bash
Verify environment variables: docker exec <container> env
fix: Ensure the main process runs in foreground mode with CMD or ENTRYPOINT directive
Add proper error handling in entrypoint scripts with set -e and trap commands
Mount required configuration files as volumes
Use --restart=no during debugging to prevent restart loops
prevention: Test containers locally with same environment as production
Implement comprehensive logging in entrypoint scripts
Use healthchecks with appropriate intervals and timeouts
Add smoke tests in CI pipeline to validate container startup

title: Containers cannot communicate on default bridge network
symptoms: Container A cannot resolve Container B by container name
Connection refused errors when accessing services between containers
docker network inspect shows containers on different networks
Communication works with IP addresses but not container names
root_cause: Default bridge network does not support DNS resolution between containers
Containers attached to different user-defined networks
Legacy linking (--link) deprecated and not configured
Custom DNS settings overriding Docker's internal DNS
diagnosis: Check network attachment: docker inspect <container> | grep Networks
Test DNS resolution: docker exec <container> nslookup <other-container>
List all networks: docker network ls
Inspect network configuration: docker network inspect bridge
fix: Create and use a user-defined bridge network:

```bash
docker network create app-network
docker run --network app-network --name service-a myimage
docker run --network app-network --name service-b myimage
```
Update docker-compose.yml to use custom networks:

```yaml
networks:
  app-network:
    driver: bridge
services:
  web:
    networks:
      - app-network
```
Use --link only as temporary workaround for legacy applications
Ensure all related services share the same network
prevention: Always define custom networks in docker-compose files
Document network architecture in project README
Use service discovery patterns for microservice communication
Test inter-container communication in staging environment

title: Permission denied errors when accessing mounted volumes
symptoms: Application logs show permission denied when writing to mounted directory
Container process cannot create files in bind-mounted volume
Works on local development machine but fails in production
Different UID/GID between host and container causing access issues
root_cause: Container process runs as different user than host file owner
Host directory permissions too restrictive for container user
SELinux or AppArmor blocking container access to host files
Volume mounted with read-only flag but application requires write access
diagnosis: Check container user: docker exec <container> id
Check mount options: docker inspect <container> | grep Mounts
Review security context: docker exec <container> getenforce
fix: Match container UID/GID with host directory owner:

```dockerfile
RUN useradd -u 1000 appuser
USER appuser
```
Adjust host directory permissions:

```bash
sudo chown -R 1000:1000 /path/to/volume
```
Use :z or :Z flags for SELinux contexts:

```bash
docker run -v /host/path:/container/path:z myimage
```
Run container with --user flag to specify UID
prevention: Document required UID/GID in project documentation
Use named volumes instead of bind mounts when possible
Implement entrypoint script to fix permissions on startup
Test volume mounts in CI with same user context as production

title: Container performance degraded due to CPU throttling
symptoms: Application response times increase under load
docker stats shows high CPU throttling percentage
Process runs slowly despite host having available CPU
Performance improves when CPU limits removed
root_cause: CPU limits set too low for application workload
CPU shares not properly configured among competing containers
Single-threaded application limited by single CPU quota
Host CPU contention from other processes
diagnosis: Check CPU limits: docker inspect <container> | grep Cpu
Review host CPU load: top or htop
fix: Increase CPU limits in docker-compose or run command:

```yaml
services:
  app:
    deploy:
      resources:
        limits:
          cpus: '2.0'
```
Adjust CPU shares for relative priority:

```bash
docker run --cpu-shares 512 myimage
```
Use --cpuset-cpus to bind to specific cores
Remove CPU limits if not strictly required
prevention: Profile application CPU requirements before setting limits
Use resource quotas in Kubernetes for production workloads
Monitor CPU throttling metrics in observability platform
Implement horizontal scaling instead of vertical scaling for CPU-bound workloads

title: Docker image size too large causing slow deployments
symptoms: docker push takes excessive time to upload
Registry storage costs increasing rapidly
Pod startup slow due to large image pull times
Image exceeds registry size limits
root_cause: Including unnecessary files in build context
Not using multi-stage builds to exclude build dependencies
Large base images instead of minimal variants
Each RUN command creating a new layer without cleanup
diagnosis: Use docker scout to analyze image contents
fix: Use multi-stage builds to exclude build tools and reduce final image size
Add .dockerignore to exclude node_modules, .git, *.md, and test directories
Combine RUN commands and clean up package cache in same layer
Use alpine or distroless base images
prevention: Enforce image size limits in CI pipeline
Regularly audit images with docker scout
Use Dependabot to keep base images updated
Document optimal base image choices in team guidelines

title: Container marked healthy but application not actually ready
symptoms: Load balancer sends traffic to container before application ready
Healthcheck passes but API returns 503 errors
Container restarts due to healthcheck failures
Inconsistent healthcheck behavior across environments
root_cause: Healthcheck only checking process existence not application readiness
Healthcheck interval too short or timeout too long
Healthcheck command not testing actual application endpoints
Application ready after healthcheck starts
diagnosis: Check healthcheck status: docker inspect <container> | grep Health
Test healthcheck command manually in container
Review application logs during startup sequence
Monitor healthcheck transition from starting to healthy
fix: Implement proper healthcheck testing application readiness:

```dockerfile
HEALTHCHECK --interval=30s --timeout=10s --start-period=60s --retries=3 \
  CMD curl -f http://localhost:8080/health || exit 1
```
Add start-period to allow application initialization time
Use application-specific health endpoints that check dependencies
Adjust interval and timeout based on application characteristics
prevention: Design health endpoints that check critical dependencies
Document healthcheck requirements in service contract
Test healthcheck behavior in load scenarios
Use readiness probes in Kubernetes for more sophisticated checks

title: Cannot pull images from private registry due to authentication failures
symptoms: docker pull returns 'unauthorized: authentication required'
CI/CD pipeline fails during image pull step
Works locally but fails in production environment
Authentication works intermittently
root_cause: Docker login credentials expired or not present on target machine
Using wrong credential store or configuration
Registry token has insufficient permissions
Credential helper not properly configured for CI/CD
diagnosis: Check docker login status: docker login
Test authentication manually with curl or registry CLI
Review CI/CD logs for authentication errors
fix: Re-authenticate with registry:

```bash
docker login registry.example.com -u <username> -p <password>
```
Use credential store for secure token management:

```bash
docker-credential-pass configure
```
For CI/CD, use registry-specific authentication:

```bash
echo $REGISTRY_PASSWORD | docker login -u $REGISTRY_USER --password-stdin registry.example.com
```
Verify token has appropriate pull permissions
prevention: Use short-lived tokens with rotation policies
Implement credential management in CI/CD with secret stores
Document registry authentication procedures
Use service accounts for automated deployments

title: IPv6 connectivity issues in containerized applications
symptoms: Applications cannot reach IPv6-only services
Container has no IPv6 address assigned
IPv6 traffic blocked between containers
DNS resolution fails for AAAA records
root_cause: Docker daemon not configured with IPv6 enabled
Host system IPv6 not enabled or misconfigured
Network driver does not support IPv6
Firewall rules blocking IPv6 traffic
diagnosis: Inspect container IP addresses: docker inspect <container> | grep IPAddress
Test IPv6 connectivity: docker exec <container> ping6 -c 3 ipv6.google.com
Verify host IPv6: ip -6 addr show
fix: Enable IPv6 in Docker daemon configuration with ipv6: true and fixed-cidr-v6
Restart Docker daemon using systemctl restart docker
Create IPv6-enabled networks with docker network create command
Update firewall rules to allow IPv6 traffic
prevention: Enable IPv6 in daemon configuration from the start
Document IPv6 requirements in project documentation
Test IPv6 connectivity in staging environment
Use dual-stack networking for compatibility

title: Docker images failing security scans with vulnerabilities
symptoms: Security scan failing with high/critical vulnerabilities
CI pipeline blocked by security policy
Base images containing known CVEs
Third-party packages with security issues
root_cause: Using outdated base images
Not updating dependencies regularly
Installing unnecessary packages
Not running vulnerability scans regularly
diagnosis: Run docker scan to identify vulnerabilities
Check base image version and release notes
fix: Update base image to latest secure version
Update vulnerable packages in Dockerfile
Use minimal base images like alpine or distroless
Implement automated security scanning in CI
prevention: Use Dependabot or similar tools for base image updates
Run security scans on every build
Implement vulnerability patching policies
Use signed and verified images

title: Docker build context too large causing slow builds
symptoms: Docker build sending large context to daemon
Builds taking excessive time
Network bandwidth consumed during build
Docker daemon disk filling up
root_cause: .dockerignore not configured or incomplete
Build context includes unnecessary files
Building from root directory of large project
Node_modules or dependencies included in context
diagnosis: Review docker build output for context size
List files being sent in build context
fix: Create or update .dockerignore file:

node_modules
.git
*.md
test
.env
Build from specific subdirectory
Use .dockerignore patterns to exclude unnecessary files
prevention: Always use .dockerignore in projects
Document build context best practices
Monitor build context size in CI
Use multi-stage builds to reduce context

title: Containers not responding to SIGTERM gracefully
symptoms: Container killed immediately on stop
Data loss during container shutdown
Database connections not closed properly
In-flight requests dropped
root_cause: Application not handling SIGTERM signal
Using ENTRYPOINT script that doesn't pass signals
Process not running in foreground
Signal handler not implemented in application
fix: Implement signal handler in application code
Use exec form of CMD/ENTRYPOINT to pass signals
Add STOPSIGNAL directive in Dockerfile
Use --stop-timeout for graceful shutdown
prevention: Always implement graceful shutdown in applications
Test shutdown procedures in CI
Use exec form for ENTRYPOINT/CMD
Document shutdown behavior

title: Overlay2 driver performance issues on high I/O workloads
symptoms: Container filesystem operations slow
High I/O wait on Docker host
Container startup delays
Disk performance degradation
root_cause: Overlay2 overhead for layered filesystem
Too many layers in image
High file creation/deletion rate
Disk not optimized for overlay filesystem
diagnosis: Analyze layer count with docker history
fix: Reduce number of layers in Dockerfile
Use volumes for high I/O directories
Consider alternative storage drivers
Optimize disk for overlay filesystem performance
prevention: Minimize layers in Dockerfiles
Use volumes for write-heavy workloads
Monitor storage driver performance
Test storage driver for workload

title: Privileged containers creating security risks
symptoms: Containers running with --privileged flag
Containers having host access
Security audit failures
Potential container escape vulnerabilities
root_cause: Using --privileged flag unnecessarily
Misunderstanding container isolation requirements
Legacy applications requiring privileged access
Copy-pasting insecure Docker run commands
diagnosis: Review Docker Compose files for privileged mode
fix: Remove --privileged flag if not required
Use specific capabilities instead of privileged:
docker run --cap-add=NET_ADMIN --cap-add=SYS_TIME
Implement proper security contexts
prevention: Never use --privileged in production
Use least privilege capabilities
Implement security policies
Audit container configurations regularly

title: Docker daemon resource exhaustion causing failures
symptoms: Docker commands hanging or timing out
Container operations failing
Docker daemon consuming excessive resources
System becoming unresponsive
root_cause: Too many containers running simultaneously
Docker daemon memory leak
Insufficient system resources
Container resource limits not set
fix: Stop unnecessary containers
Restart Docker daemon
Increase system resources
Set resource limits on containers
prevention: Monitor Docker daemon resource usage
Set resource limits on containers
Implement container lifecycle management
Regularly clean up unused containers

title: Multi-architecture Docker builds failing
symptoms: Buildx multi-arch builds failing
Images not working on target architecture
Cross-compilation errors
Platform-specific runtime failures
root_cause: Buildx not properly configured for multi-arch
QEMU not available for emulation
Platform-specific dependencies in code
Incorrect base image architecture
diagnosis: Check buildx builders: docker buildx ls
fix: Create buildx builder with multi-arch support:
docker buildx create --use --driver=docker-container --name=multiarch-builder
Enable QEMU for emulation: docker run --rm --privileged multiarch/qemu-user-static --reset -p yes
Use platform-agnostic base images
prevention: Test multi-arch builds regularly
Use platform-agnostic dependencies
Document multi-arch build process
Use buildx for cross-platform builds

title: Rootless Docker experiencing permission issues
symptoms: Rootless Docker daemon failing to start
Permission denied errors on volume mounts
Unable to bind privileged ports
Container networking issues
root_cause: User namespaces not configured
Incorrect UID/GID mappings
Missing subuid/subgid configuration
Network setup issues for rootless mode
fix: Configure subuid/subgid for user
Set correct UID/GID mappings
Use slirp4netns for user-mode networking
Follow rootless Docker installation guide
prevention: Follow official rootless Docker setup guide
Document rootless configuration
Test rootless setup thoroughly
Use supported distributions
